#### (a) — `toFunctionField` injectivity gives `f ≠ 0`

(or, if a slightly weaker form serves, `..._toFunctionField_ne_zero_of_ne_zero`).

Proof sketch (in prose, NO Lean tactics):

The `toFunctionField` map factors as the composition of four
project-/Mathlib-side maps each of which is injective on the integral
scheme `C`:

1. `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` :

5. `Subtype.val` : `↥(carrierSubmoduleSheaf ⊤) → K(C)`, injective on

Composing the four LinearEquivs + the final `Subtype.val` injection
gives `toFunctionField` as a `kbar`-linear injection

127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:

90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}

93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).

240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:

358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the

361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.

378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).

388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
393	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
394	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
395	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
396	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
397	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
398	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
399	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
400	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
401	  and only if:
402	
403	  \begin{itemize}
404	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
405	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
406	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
407	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
408	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
409	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
410	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
411	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
412	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
413	  \end{itemize}
414	  In divisorial language (REF,
415	  REF), the two conditions combine to
416	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
417	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
418	  zero global section.
419	
420	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
421	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
422	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
423	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
424	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
425	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
426	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
427	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
428	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
429	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
430	\end{proof}

432	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
433	
434	\begin{lemma}
435	
436	
437	\leanok
438	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
439	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
441	  
442	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
443	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
444	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
445	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
446	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
447	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
448	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
449	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
450	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
451	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
452	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
453	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
455	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
456	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
457	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
458	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
459	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
460	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
461	  \[
462	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
463	  \]
464	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
465	\end{lemma}
466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  
469	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
470	  exact sequence
471	  \[
472	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
473	  \]
474	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
475	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
476	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
477	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
478	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
479	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
480	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
481	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
482	  \[
483	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
484	  \]
485	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
486	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
487	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
488	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
489	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
490	  \begin{multline*}
491	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
492	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
493	  \end{multline*}
494	  Two ingredients close the proof:
495	
496	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
497	  hypothesis: by REF,
498	  \[
499	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
500	  \]
501	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
502	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
503	
504	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
505	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
506	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
507	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
508	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
509	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
510	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
511	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
512	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
513	
514	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
515	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
516	  \[
517	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
518	  \]
519	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
520	
521	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
522	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
523	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
524	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
525	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
526	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
527	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
528	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
529	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
530	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
531	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
532	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
533	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
534	  complex degenerates).
535	\end{proof}

632	
633	\begin{corollary}
634

1008	  this stalk, so `f = ι(s)` satisfies `f_P · f ∈ 𝒪_{C, P}`, i.e.

\subsection{Iter-195 substrate substeps for the
  non-constant-rational corollary}
\label{subsec:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_substrate_substeps}

The iter-195 substrate prover reduced the body of
\cref{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero} to a
private helper \texttt{exists\_nonconstant\_rational\_from\_dim\_eq\_two}
(file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}, line~1323)
which carries the substantive linear-algebra plus principal-divisor
content of the corollary, isolated from the genus hypothesis. The
helper's body, after the iter-195 push, executes six structural setup
steps culminating in the choice of a non-constant candidate section
\(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\setminus \bar k\cdot s_1\) (with
\(s_1\) the distinguished constant section satisfying
\(\iota(s_1)=1\)) and the definition
\[
  f \;:=\; \iota(s) \;=\;
  \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}\,P\,h_P\,h_{P\mathrm{coh}}\,s
  \;\in\; K(C).
\]
After this setup, three named sub-claims (a), (b), (c) remain to close
the helper. The iter-196 plan splits each into a project-bespoke
substrate lemma carrying its own \(\lean\)-pin and proof recipe. The
sub-claims are Archon-original decompositions of the helper's
in-Lean documentation block at lines~1421--1440 of
\texttt{OCofP.lean}; the underlying mathematics is the standard
content of Hartshorne~IV.1 Exercise~1.1 (already cited at the
\(\cref{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}\)
level) augmented by the Stacks Project tag \texttt{02P0} input cited
inline in sub-claim~(c).

\begin{lemma}
[Sub-claim (a): \(\texttt{toFunctionField}\) is \(\bar k\)-linearly
injective, hence preserves non-zero-ness of sections]
  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField_injective}
  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField,
        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf,
        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
  over \(\bar k\) and let \(P\in C\) be a closed point with coheight
  witness \(h_{P\mathrm{coh}}\colon \mathrm{coheight}(P)=1\). Then the
  \(\bar k\)-linear map
  \[
    \iota \;:=\;
    \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}\,P\,h_P\,h_{P\mathrm{coh}}
    \;\colon\;
    H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;\longrightarrow\; K(C)
  \]
  of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField} is injective.
  In particular, for any global section \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\)
  with \(s\ne 0\), the rational function \(f:=\iota(s)\in K(C)\) is
  non-zero.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff,
        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
  The map \(\iota\) factors as the composition of four \(\bar k\)-linear
  isomorphisms and one final injective \(\bar k\)-linear map; each
  factor's injectivity is delivered by existing Mathlib or
  project-substrate hooks. Concretely, on the integral scheme \(C\),
  \(\iota\) equals the composition:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item \(\texttt{HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv}_{\bar k,\,\mathcal O_C(P)}\):
      the project's $\bar k$-linear isomorphism
      $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \texttt{Scheme.HModule}\,
      \bar k\,\mathcal O_C(P)\,0
        \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
        \Hom_{\Sh}(\mathbf 1, \mathcal O_C(P))$
      with \(\mathbf 1\) the constant sheaf of \(\bar k\) on \(C\). This
      bridge is the project-substrate
      \texttt{Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean} entry
      \texttt{HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv}.
    \item \texttt{sheafToPresheaf.map}: the fully faithful functor
      \(\Sheaf \to \Psh\) on sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules sends the
      sheaf-hom of layer~(1) to the underlying presheaf-hom; full
      faithfulness is Mathlib's
      \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.fullyFaithfulSheafToPresheaf}, so
      this step is injective on hom-sets.
    \item \(\texttt{constantSheafAdj}_{\bar k,\,\mathcal O_C(P)}.\texttt{homEquiv}\):
      Mathlib's constant-sheaf-adjunction
      \(\texttt{constantSheaf} \dashv \Gamma\) supplies the
      \(\bar k\)-linear isomorphism
      $\Hom_{\Psh}(\mathbf 1_{\mathrm{psh}}, \mathcal O_C(P).\val)
       \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
       \Hom_{\ModuleCat\,\bar k}(\bar k, \mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top))$.
    \item Evaluation at the generator \(1\in\bar k\): the canonical
      \(\bar k\)-linear isomorphism
      $\Hom_{\ModuleCat\,\bar k}(\bar k, M)
       \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
       M, \qquad \varphi \mapsto \varphi(1),$
      with \(M:=\mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top)\) the global-section module
      of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}. The
      Mathlib name at the project's pinned commit may be
      \texttt{LinearMap.applyOneEquiv} or its
      \texttt{LinearEquiv.fromBaseRing} variant; the prover hand-rolls
      a 5-line \texttt{LinearEquiv} if the exact name has been
      renamed upstream.
    \item \texttt{Subtype.val}: the carrier sheaf
      \(\mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top) \;=\;
      \texttt{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}\,P\,h_{P\mathrm{coh}}(\op\,\top)\)
      is a \(\bar k\)-submodule of the function field \(K(C)\)
      (\cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}); the
      coercion \texttt{Subtype.val} to \(K(C)\) is injective.
  \end{enumerate}
  Each layer (1)--(4) is a \texttt{LinearEquiv}; layer (5) is
  \texttt{Function.Injective Subtype.val}. The composition is therefore
  an injective \(\bar k\)-linear map, and this composition is precisely
  the body unfolding of
  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}: the iter-187
  refactor of \texttt{toFunctionField} is the formal record of this
  factorisation (compare the \emph{iter-187 closure} commentary at
  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField} and the
  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr} proof at line~1024 of
  \texttt{OCofP.lean}, where the same chain is unfolded in the
  reverse direction). The non-zero-transfer conclusion is immediate:
  if \(\iota(s)=0\) and \(s\ne 0\), then \(\iota\) has a non-trivial
  kernel, contradicting injectivity; equivalently, applying
  injectivity to \(\iota(s)=0=\iota(0)\) (using
  \texttt{htF\_zero}, already in scope at line~1364 of the helper
  body) gives \(s=0\), contradicting the iter-195 hypothesis
  \(s\notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) (which is strictly stronger than
  \(s\ne 0\)).

  \emph{Estimated formalisation footprint.} \(\sim 30\)--\(50\)
  Lean LOC: layer~(1) is one line (named bridge); layer~(2) is
  \(\sim 5\) LOC; layer~(3) is \(\sim 5\)--\(10\) LOC; layer~(4) is
  \(\sim 5\)--\(10\) LOC (hand-roll if necessary); layer~(5) is one
  line (\texttt{Subtype.val\_injective}); the composition and the
  final \(s\ne 0\Rightarrow\iota(s)\ne 0\) wrap each add 5--10 LOC.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sub-claim (b): order conditions on \(\iota(s)\) from
\texttt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr}]
  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_order_conditions_of_globalSection}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.order_conditions_of_globalSection}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff,
        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
  Let \(C, P, h_P, h_{P\mathrm{coh}}\) be as in
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}, let
  \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) with \(s\ne 0\), and set
  \(f:=\iota(s)\in K(C)\), so \(f\ne 0\) by
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}. Then
  \(f\) satisfies the order conditions:
  \[
    \ord_Q(f) \;\ge\; 0
    \qquad \text{for every prime divisor } Q\in C \text{ with } Q\ne P,
    \qquad
    \ord_P(f) \;\ge\; -1.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
  Apply the backward direction
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
  (the \(\mathtt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr}\) helper at line~1024 of
  \texttt{OCofP.lean}) with the existence witness
  \(\langle s, \mathtt{rfl}\rangle\) supplied by the definition
  \(f=\iota(s)\): the backward direction takes a hypothesis of the
  shape ``there exists \(s'\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) with
  \(\iota(s')=f\)'' and returns the conjunction of the two order
  conditions stated. With our \(s\) and the equation
  \texttt{hf\_def : f = $\iota$(s)} (the iter-195 setup at line~1418 of
  \texttt{OCofP.lean}), the witness is literally
  \(\langle s,\, \texttt{hf\_def.symm}\rangle\), and the helper returns
  the pair of order bounds in one step.

  Note: the \(\mathtt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr}\) helper itself is
  already landed (line~1024 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}) and is kernel-clean
  modulo the upstream \(\texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}\) /
  \(\texttt{toFunctionField}\) body sorries; both of those bodies are
  themselves landed at iter-187 closure. The iter-196 substrate
  declaration
  \(\texttt{order\_conditions\_of\_globalSection}\) is therefore a
  one-line invocation of the existing helper with the witness as
  above; the projected formalisation footprint is \(\sim 10\)
  LOC, dominated by the typeclass alignment between the local
  \texttt{hf} hypothesis (non-vanishing of \(f\)) supplied by
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective} and
  the signature of \(\mathtt{globalSections\_iff\_mpr}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sub-claim (c): principal divisor of a non-constant rational function
on a complete curve is non-zero]
  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_ne_zero_of_nonconstant}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective,
        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_order_conditions_of_globalSection}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P\in C\) be a
  closed point with coheight witness, let
  \(s\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) be a global section satisfying
  \(s\notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) (with \(s_1\) the distinguished constant
  section, iter-195 setup line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}), and set
  \(f:=\iota(s)\) with \(h_f\colon f\ne 0\) supplied by
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}. Then
  the principal Weil divisor of \(f\) is non-zero:
  \[
    \texttt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,(X:=C.\mathrm{left})\,f\,h_f
    \;\ne\;
    0 \quad \text{in } \mathrm{Div}(C.\mathrm{left}).
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}
  Argue by the contrapositive. Suppose for contradiction that
  \(\texttt{principal}\,f\,h_f = 0\). The principal divisor is
  \(\sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot [Q]\), summed over prime divisors
  \(Q\in C.\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}\); the vanishing of the principal
  divisor is therefore equivalent to \(\ord_Q(f)=0\) for every prime
  divisor \(Q\) of \(C\). Hence \(f\) has no zeros and no poles on
  \(C\), and the inclusion
  \(\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\) places \(f\) inside
  the subsheaf of regular functions on \(C\), i.e.\
  \(f\in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)\). On a complete geometrically
  irreducible integral scheme over the algebraically closed field
  \(\bar k\) (in particular: smooth proper integral curve), the
  ring of global sections of the structure sheaf is exactly the base
  field,
  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks
  Project tag \texttt{01XU}); the non-vanishing \(f\ne 0\) plus the
  fact that all elements of \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}(\top)\) coincide
  with \(\bar k^{\times}\) (the units refinement, Stacks Project tag
  \texttt{02P0}) places \(f\in \bar k^{\times}\subset K(C)\).

  We now derive a contradiction from the hypothesis
  \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Set \(c:=f\in\bar k\), so
  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot 1 = 0\) in \(K(C)\), i.e.\
  \(\iota(s) - c \cdot \iota(s_1) = 0\) (since \(\iota(s_1)=1\), the
  defining equation \texttt{hs1 : toFunctionField s\(_1\) = 1} at
  line~1355 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}). By the \(\bar k\)-linearity of
  \(\iota\) (witnesses \texttt{htF\_smul} at line~1369 and
  \texttt{htF\_add} at line~1378 of the iter-195 helper body),
  \(\iota(s - c\cdot s_1) = 0\). By
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}, the
  map \(\iota\) is injective, so \(s - c\cdot s_1 = 0\), i.e.\
  \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting the iter-195
  hypothesis \(s\notin\bar k\cdot s_1\). Hence \(\texttt{principal}\,
  f\,h_f \ne 0\), as required.

  \emph{Mathlib substrate consumed.} The statement
  ``global sections of \(\mathcal O_C\) on a complete integral curve
  over \(\bar k\) are exactly \(\bar k\)'' is the Hartshorne~I.3.4 /
  Stacks~\texttt{01XU} input; its units refinement (Stacks~\texttt{02P0})
  is direct since the constants \(\bar k\) form a field. At the
  pinned Mathlib commit the relevant entry-point is the
  project's existing function-field-of-curve identification + the
  proper + integral + algebraically-closed-base typeclass package
  available in the helper's local context. If a self-contained
  project-side lemma \texttt{Scheme.functionField\_of\_complete\_curve\_const}
  (or equivalent) is not yet available, the iter-196 prover lands a
  small named helper for this fact (estimated \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) LOC
  for the Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement) and consumes it here.

  \emph{Estimated formalisation footprint.} \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) Lean
  LOC for the contrapositive setup, the
  ``no-zeros-or-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' step, the constants
  identification, and the final linear-independence contradiction.
  The kbar-linearity witnesses (\texttt{htF\_smul},
  \texttt{htF\_add}, \texttt{htF\_zero}) are already in scope
  (lines~1364--1387 of \texttt{OCofP.lean}); the injectivity hypothesis
  is consumed from
  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}.
\end{proof}

\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
